coredata.dc:title: Cellulose nanofiber-based pH indicator integrated with resazurin-modified carbon dots for real-time monitoring of food freshness
coredata.dc:creator: Ezati, Parya
Khan, Ajahar
Rhim, Jong-Whan
coredata.dc:description: Modified carbon dots with resazurin (R-CD) were prepared using a hydrothermal method to fabricate a CNF-based pH-responsive indicator for real-time food freshness monitoring. FE-SEM and FTIR results confirmed that the R-CD was uniformly distributed in the CNF matrix. The thermal stability of the CNF/R-CD indicator film was increased, and the water contact angle of the film was slightly lower than that of the neat CNF film. The CNF/R-CD film showed excellent UV-barrier properties, with ∼98.3% and 87.7% blocking the UV-B and UV-A light. The indicator film showed excellent color stability even after 2 months of storage at 25 °C and responded rapidly to alkaline pH and NH3 through a color change from yellow to brownish-red. The CNF/R-CD indicator films exhibited distinct color changes during shrimp packaging depending on pH and TVB-N concentration. Therefore, the CNF/R-CD indicator can be used as a promising pH-sensitive color-changing intelligent packaging application for freshness monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Color-changing
Smart indicator
Carbon dots
Shrimp packaging
pH-responsive

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensitivity of color change to acid and ammonia gas
Water contact angle (WCA) and water vapor permeability (WVP)
Color stability of R-CDs-based indicator films
Application of indicator for shrimp packaging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government ( MSIT ) ( 2022R1A2B5B02001422 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM images of the surface and cross-sectional views of the CNF-based films. (b) elemental mapping and (c) EDX spectra of the pure-CNF and CNF/R-CD3% film, respectively.
XPS full survey scan of the neat CNF and CNF/R-CD3% films (a,b), and corresponding high-resolution scans of C 1s (c,d), O 1s (e,f), and N 1s (g,h).
Release rate of the R-CD from the CNF-based films in various food simulant solutions.

Images of the CNF/R-CD3% indicator film after being subjected to various buffer solutions (a), with their color values (b). Images of the CNF/R-CD3% indicator film (c) with the color values (d) after being subjected to fumes of ammonia and acid. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Changes in the chemical analysis of shrimp samples and color values of the CNF/R-CD3% indicator film at 25 °C for 24 h. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Monitoring the freshness of shrimp using the CNF/R-CD3% indicator at 25 °C and (b) schematic presentation of the indicator's responsive color shift. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Cellulose nanofiber-based pH indicator integrated with resazurin-modified carbon dots for real-time monitoring of food freshness

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul, 02447, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul, 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Modified carbon dots with resazurin (R-CD) were prepared using a hydrothermal method to fabricate a CNF-based pH-responsive indicator for real-time food freshness monitoring. FE-SEM and FTIR results confirmed that the R-CD was uniformly distributed in the CNF matrix. The thermal stability of the CNF/R-CD indicator film was increased, and the water contact angle of the film was slightly lower than that of the neat CNF film. The CNF/R-CD film showed excellent UV-barrier properties, with ∼98.3% and 87.7% blocking the UV-B and UV-A light. The indicator film showed excellent color stability even after 2 months of storage at 25 °C and responded rapidly to alkaline pH and NH3 through a color change from yellow to brownish-red. The CNF/R-CD indicator films exhibited distinct color changes during shrimp packaging depending on pH and TVB-N concentration. Therefore, the CNF/R-CD indicator can be used as a promising pH-sensitive color-changing intelligent packaging application for freshness monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Globally, the food packaging business has developed to suit consumer expectations and fulfill roles beyond the fundamental ones that packaging originally served (containment, protection, usefulness, and information). In the last 20 years, the phrase "smart packaging" (which includes active and intelligent packaging) has gained popularity. It refers to more inventive packaging with more functions (Jafarzadeh et al., 2021). The main purposes of intelligent packaging are to monitor the state of packaged food and deliver real-time information on quality throughout transit and storage. A type of intelligent packaging called a freshness indicator offers real-time, non-intrusive, and non-destructive food quality and safety monitoring. The Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) study found that each year, nearly one-third of the food produced for human use globally is wasted (Kilic et al., 2022). Since most food loss occurs during distribution and consumption, freshness indicators can reduce food waste, improve sustainability and protect consumers from food disease (Zhang et al., 2020). Nanotechnology has emerged as a viable method for food safety sensing thanks to immediate advances in nanoscience, offering the potential to overcome many food safety challenges. The advantages of nanomaterial-based sensors over conventional sensing technologies include more accurate target recognition and significantly improved selectivity and sensitivity (Fu et al., 2022). Sensing performance is greatly affected by nanomaterials, which are the main part of nanosensors. It is increasingly important to produce inexpensive nanomaterials to improve sensing performance. Many efficient nanomaterials, including metal nanoparticles, semiconductors, and carbon-based materials, have been produced for food safety applications. Due to their excellent properties, nanomaterials have been widely used in chemical and biological sensing, bioimaging, and nanomedicine (Harish et al., 2022). CDs can be used in various industries, such as solar cells, bioimaging, and drug delivery, due to their excellent properties, such as low toxicity, biocompatibility, chemical stability, and photostability (Zhao et al., 2022). Also, the CD is safe with little cytotoxicity, even at high concentrations. In most cases, cytotoxicity studies of CD performed to date show that a concentration of 100 μg/mL causes only a slight loss in cell viability, which is expected to be safe for packaging use (Khan et al., 2023). CDs are particularly interesting in pH sensors because they are easy to detect, respond quickly, and are highly sensitive (Khan et al., 2023).

In general, the optical properties of CDs can be changed by some molecular or environmental factors. For example, CDs can change color in response to certain food quality indices such as pH, water activity, or gas production. Modifying the surface of CDs can increase their sensitivity and specificity as a targeting detection system (Khan et al., 2023). Many studies have shown the efficacy of CD for this purpose. For example, fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) has been combined with polyethyleneimine-modified CD to create a CD that can monitor the pH changes in foods such as yogurt (Zhu et al., 2017). It is interesting to note that several recent research works have suggested that CDs can be utilized as sensors to measure the pH of foods (Fu et al., 2022; Yu et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2020). Therefore, CDs are suitable for applications as sensors because they can change their fluorescence signal depending on interactions with specific elements in their surroundings. However, the most frequently reported CDs exhibit a limited range of pH responses and are often unsuitable for representing quality changes in real foods requiring clear identification. Therefore, developing a high-contrast sensor that can detect pH changes with the naked eye is important.

In this context, resazurin could be of great interest in developing pH sensors, especially in investigating its potential use in intelligent food packaging. Resazurin, a non-toxic substance with a shimmering purple color, is widely used to assess cell viability (Nishiyama et al., 1998). An oxidation-reduction reaction converts resazurin to its brightly fluorescent derivative, resorufin (pink) (Madushan et al., 2021). In an acidic environment, resorufin forms the caproyl ester, a colorless, non-fluorescent hexanoyl resorufin. Ammonia and biogenic amines react with these caproyl ester groups to produce resorufin, a very light pink hue (Chen et al., 2012). Here, we developed modified carbon dots with resazurin (R-CD) for quick, simple, and affordable pH change sensing. The new indicator exhibits a more vivid red-orange color under alkaline conditions, making it more advantageous for alkaline biogenic amine detection.

Polymer matrices are a good choice for intelligent packaging applications. Utilizing a polymer matrix as a background to enhance the human eye's ability to perceive color changes is a typical feature of smart labels. Of particular interest in terms of rapid analysis are multichannel cellulose nanofiber (CNF) based indicators. CNF consists of small cellulose fibers with a large aspect ratio which enables gases and water vapor to interact with the trapped pigments and alter their hue (Ezati et al., 2021). The suitability of CNF for food packaging is confirmed by its biocompatibility, low cost, biodegradability, and non-toxicity (Zhang et al., 2021). Therefore, in this study, we aimed to develop a freshness indicator by fixing CDs and resazurin (R-CD) to a CNF matrix for smart packaging applications that can monitor food quality in real time through the pH-dependent color change of the indicator. The indicator was attached inside the headspace without directly contacting the sample in the packaging test. CDs can be used as fillers for making intelligent packaging films. Optionally, the CD may be placed in a bag and wrapped in packaging film to prevent direct contact with the sample. Therefore, CD migration from film to sample is not risky in this study.

CNF (TEMPO-CNF, length: 3–350 μm; width: 10–20 nm) was provided from Daejung Chemicals & Metals Co. Ltd. Resazurin (C12H7NO4) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Phosphate buffer solutions with a pH range of 4–10 were bought from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Chelmsford, MA, USA). Fresh shrimp (Chinese white shrimp, 15–18 cm) were procured live from a local market (Seoul, Korea) for the packing test.

A one-step hydrothermal procedure was employed to make R-CD utilizing tangerine peel and resazurin. For this, 0.3 g of tangerine peel powder and 0.001 g of resazurin were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water while being stirred, put into a 100 mL stainless steel reactor, and then heated to 160 °C for 6 h while reacting. Unreacted components were eliminated by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm (12298×g) for 30 min at room temperature (22 ± 2 °C) after chilling the reaction result. To get rid of contaminants, the supernatant was filtered via a Whatman membrane filter (25 mm diameter, 0.2 m pore size, Whatman, Maidstone, UK). The filtrate was kept in a refrigerator (4 °C) for further testing.

Various quantities of R-CD (1.0%, 2.0%, and 3.0% based on CNF) were dissolved in distilled water to create CNF-based indicator films. Separately, distilled water was used to create CNF suspension (1% w/v) under gentle mechanical agitation at 50 °C. Glycerol was then added as a plasticizer along with further stirring for a further 20 min at 50 °C. The film-forming solution was then added as a color indicator at the preset concentration of R-CD (1.0%, 2.0%, and 3.0% w/v) and stirred continuously for 1 h at room temperature (22 ± 2 °C). Finally, 200 mL of each film-forming solution was manually poured onto Teflon-coated glass plates measuring 24 × 30 cm2 and allowed to dry for 48 h at room temperature (22 ± 2 °C). The dry films were conditioned for at least 48 h in a humidity chamber at 25 °C and 50% RH. The R-CD-free version of the film was created as the control. Depending on the R-CD concentration, the manufactured films were labeled CNF (control), CNF/R-CD1%, CNF/R-CD2%, and CNF/R-CD3%.

The film samples' surface and cross-sectional morphology, along with the EDX, was investigated using a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, SU 8010, Hitachi Co., Ltd., S4 Matsuda, Japan) equipped with energy dispersive X-ray (EDAX) (S.No. 56756, Oxford instruments INCA, X.act, High Wycombe, UK) with at an accelerating voltage of 8 kV.

The chemical composition and types of functional groups were determined by recording the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra through a K-alpha X-Ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Co. Ltd., Waltham, Massachusetts, USA) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra using, FTIR spectrometer, (ATR PRO 4X FTIR spectrophotometer, Jasco, Japan) in the range of 500–4000 cm−1, respectively. Using a thermogravimetric analyzer, the thermal stabilities of the indicator films were evaluated (Hi-Res TGA 2950, TA Instrument, New Castle, DE, USA). For this, a test sample weighing about 10 mg was heated in an aluminum pan at a scanning heating rate of 10 °C/min while being exposed to a nitrogen flow of 50 cm3/min in the temperature range of 25–600 °C.
The surface color of the film was determined using a colorimeter (Konica Minolta CR-400 Chromameter, Tokyo, Japan).

A UV–vis spectrophotometer (Mecasys Optizen POP Series UV/Vis, Seoul, Korea) was used to record the UV–Vis light transmission spectra of the films in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The following formulae were used to get the UV-blocking(%) for UV-A (320–400 nm) and UV-B (280–320 nm):
(1)
U V − A
b l o c k i n g
( % )
= 100 −
∫ 320 400
T
( λ )
d λ
∫ 320 400
d λ
(2)
U V − B
b l o c k i n g
( % )
= 100 −
∫ 280 320
T
( λ )
d λ
∫ 280 320
d λ
where T(λ) is the average transmittance of the film at the wavelength λ, and dλ is the bandwidth of the film.

Mechanical properties were investigated through Instron Universal Testing Machine (Model 5565, Instron Engineering Corporation, Canton, MA, USA) as per the standard protocol of ASTM D 882-88 to determine the tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB).
As previously reported, the water vapor permeability (WVP) of the films made in accordance with ASTM E96-95 was measured using the gravimetric cup technique (Ezati et al., 2021).

The contact angle analyzer (Phoneix 150, Surface Electro-Optics Co., Ltd., Kunpo, Korea) was used to measure the surface wettability or water contact angle (WCA) of the tested materials, as previously stated (Ezati et al., 2021).

By modifying the procedures described elsewhere (Razavi et al., 2020), the quantity of R-CD released from the CNF/R-CD1%, CNF/R-CD2%, and CNF/R-CD3% films by food stimulants was conducted. In order to do this, 20 mL of various food simulant solutions (10%, 50%, and 95% ethanol, as well as water) were added to a film specimen (2 cm × 2 cm), and the testing samples were held at 25 °C with moderate shaking. Then, using a UV–vis spectrophotometer, 1 mL of the solution was used to measure the absorbance at 395 nm at predefined intervals (0 min, 30 min, 60 min, 90 min, 120 min, 150 min, and 180 min).

The color-changing and pH-sensitivity function of the CNF/R-CD3% indicator film was evaluated following the method of Ezati et al. (2021) with slight modifications. The CNF-R indicator films were immersed in different buffer solutions (pH ranging from 4 to 10). Film images were then captured to show the color change.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of color change to acid and ammonia gas
ce:section.ce:para: The color change function of the film was tested using ammonia and acetic acid gases to evaluate the color change sensitivity of the film as a function of pH (Ezati et al., 2021). To this end, samples of the color-indicating film were cut into square shapes (3 cm × 3 cm), fixed in Erlenmeyer flasks containing ammonia (2 M, 50 mL) or acetic acid (18 M, 50 mL) solutions, and held for 15 min at 25 °C. The distance between the film and ammonia/acetic acid solutions was 2 cm. A digital camera captured the indicator's image, and the color indicator film's color values were also recorded using a colorimeter.

The color stability of the indicator films was evaluated over time with film specimens stored at room temperature (22 ± 2 °C) for 60 days. The color change of the indicators during the storage period was evaluated and compared with the initial color values.

The CNF/R-CD3% indicator film was used to monitor shrimps' spoilage during storage. A square film sample (3 cm × 3 cm) was mounted to the inner side of the packaging lid of the sterilized container (100 cm × 100 cm × 5.5 cm) containing 100 g of fresh shrimps and stored at 25 °C for 24 h. The shrimp sample's color properties were measured using a colorimeter. Film samples were tested for a similar experiment three consecutive times to confirm the color indicator's proficiency. The steam distillation method was used to determine the release of the total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) during shrimp storage (Ezati et al., 2021). TVB-N was extracted using a Kjeldahl distillation unit, collected in an Erlenmeyer flask, and then the amount of TVB-N was assessed via H2SO4 titration. The TVB-N concentration was then indicated as mg/100 g sample (mg%).
For the pH measurement, 10 g of the test shrimp was blended with 90 mL of distilled water, and the pH of the resulting slurry was calculated using a digital pH meter (Thermo Scientific, Indonesia).

FE-SEM analyzed the surface morphology of the CNF-based films, and the results are shown in Fig. 1 a. The surface image of the neat CNF film showed that the films were smooth without any cracks or voids. When 1 wt% R-CD was added (CNF/R-CD1% film), the surface appearance did not change as the surface was similar to the neat CNF film. The microstructure of the CNF/R-CD2% and CNF/R-CD3% films appear more intact, smoother, and free from voids or cracks than the neat CNF film. However, R-CDs were stably dispersed throughout the film matrix, although some small clumps with a more curved appearance were observed for the CNF/R-CD2% and CNF/R-CD3% films. The compact structure of the composite films with a high amount of R-CD loading suggests that the fillers were compatible with the CNF matrix since R-CD loading increases the surface uniformity of the composite film without forming distinctive aggregations. FE-SEM images of the cross-sections also showed an integral appearance with no pores, ruptures, voids, or cavities, suggesting good dispersion of R-CDs in the CNF matrix. Elemental mapping images and EDX spectra for the pure CNF and CNF/R-CD3% film are shown in Fig. 1b and c. The EDX spectrum confirms the existence of carbon and oxygen as the main constitutional elements in the pure-CNF film. However, an additional nitrogen peak was also observed for CNF/R-CD3% film, indicating that R-CD was well incorporated into the polymer matrix. Moreover, the mapping images suggest that the dispersion of the individual elements is uniform, again confirming the uniform mixing of the R-CD and CNF matrix.

The XPS analysis was performed to probe the chemical composition and existence of the constitutional elements of as-prepared CNF-based films. Fig. 2 a and b shows the full-survey spectra and high-resolution XPS of the neat CNF and CNF/R-CD3% films. The 284.52 eV, 398.97 eV, and 530.69 eV peaks indicate carbon (C 1s of sp2 C), N 1s, and O 1s of the surface functional groups, respectively (Zhao et al., 2015). The results obtained by the XPS were in good agreement with the EDX results. The high-resolution XPS C1s spectrum of both films (Fig. 2c and d) can be deconvoluted into four component peaks located at ∼283.98 eV (reflects the bonding structure of C–H or C–C bonds), ∼284.99 eV, and ∼285.78 eV (attributed to the C–N and C–O bonds) and ∼287.18 eV (corresponds to the O–C–O or CO of carbonyl) (Zhao et al., 2015). Similarly, the high-resolution XPS O 1s spectrum of the neat CNF and CNF/R-CD3% films (Fig. 2e and f) can be deconvoluted into three peaks of ∼530.2 eV, ∼531.8 eV, and ∼532.4 eV, attributed to C–O/N–O, CO, and C–O–H groups, respectively (Li et al., 2015; Zhao et al., 2015). Further, the deconvoluted XPS N1s spectra of the neat CNF film resolved into the fitting of three component peaks located around 398.75 eV, 399.2 eV, and 400.0 eV due to the C–N–C, tertiary N bonded to carbon atoms in the form of N-(C)3, and N–H bonds, respectively (Xia et al., 2004; Zhao et al., 2015). Compared to the neat CNF film, the fourth peak located around ∼285.1 eV may have been due to the presence of R-CD in the CNF/R-CD3% film (Fig. 2g and h).

The optical characteristics of the indicator films were investigated by recording the UV–vis light transmission spectra, as shown in Fig. S2a. The neat CNF film was freestanding, colorless, and transparent. Still, when R-CD was added, there was a significant change in the apparent color of the film due to the brown color of R-CD. The neat CNF film exhibited negligible light absorbance in the visible range, highly transparent with 87.6% ± 0.3% of light transmittance at 660 nm (T660), as shown in Fig. S2a. A slight shouldering peak in the UV range appeared around ∼280 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition of the CO group of cellulose (Kim et al., 2021). However, when R-CD was added to the CNF matrix, the visible light transmittance was greatly reduced. When 3 wt% of R-CD was added, the T660 decreased to 46.3% ± 0.3%, reducing the light transmittance by 47.1% compared to the neat film.

The neat CNF film exhibited high transmittance of UV light with a T280 of 53.1% ± 0.7% (Table S1). However, the addition of R-CD reduced the film's UV light transmittance significantly, mainly due to the UV light absorption capacity of the R-CD containing phenolic compounds. In order to investigate the UV-blocking properties of CNF-based films in more detail, UV-blocking characteristics were investigated for each type of UV, and the results are shown in Fig. S2b. The neat CNF film showed 17.0% ± 0.8% UV-A and 27.8% ± 0.7% UV-B blocking properties. On the other hand, the UV-A and UV-B blocking properties of the R-CD-added film increased significantly. CNF film with 3 wt% of R-CD blocked 98.3% ± 0.4% of UV-B and 87.8% ± 0.5% of UV-A lights. This result shows that the UV-blocking properties of CNF films can be improved by adding R-CD.

The mechanical properties of the CNF-based films were determined by measuring tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB), and the results are revealed in Fig. S3. The thickness of the CNF film slightly increased from 0.0225 ± 0.0017 mm to 0.0254 ± 0.001 mm, depending on the concentration of R-CD due to the increased solid content. The load resistance of the CNF-based film continuously increased as the concentration of R-CD increased, and the breaking force of CNF increased from 35.3 ± 2.7 to 48.4 ± 3.5 N at 3 wt% R-CD was added (Fig. S3b). The neat CNF film was relatively strong and less flexible with the TS of 62.7 ± 3.3 MPa, and the EB of 2.8 ± 0.1 mm (Figs. S3c and d). However, the TS of the film increased to 82.7 ± 3.5 MPa (∼32% increase) when 3 wt% of R-CD was added, which was attributed to the more compact structure of the CNF/R-CD3% film due to the uniformly dispersed R-CD in the CNF polymer matrix and hydrogen bonding between surface functional groups of R-CD and polymer matrix. Kousheh et al. (2020) observed a similar effect of additives on the mechanical properties of different biopolymer-based films (Kousheh et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water contact angle (WCA) and water vapor permeability (WVP)
ce:section.ce:para: The WCA and WVP of the CNF-based films are shown in Fig. S4. The WCA of the neat CNF film was 42.2 ± 1.3°, indicating it has a hydrophilic surface. The WCA of the film was slightly reduced to 42.1-40.0° by the addition of the R-CDs due to the hydrophilic nature of the CDs.

The WVP of the CNF-based films is shown in Fig. S4. The WVP of the neat CNF film was 5.65 × 10−10±2.2 × 10−11 g m/m2.Pa.s and increased slightly by the addition of the R-CDs. Nevertheless, despite the low concentration of R-CDs, the addition of R-CDs had little impact on the film's water vapor barrier capabilities. Previously, it was reported that the addition of naphthoquinone pigment (colorant) at a low level (<2 wt%) did not affect the WVP of the agar-based color indicator film, but the WVP of the film increased significantly as the concentration of the additive increased (Huang et al., 2019).

Functional properties or the cytotoxicity of the functional packaging films in the food packaging application mainly rely on the additives' release properties from packaging films. Several studies on the release behavior of nanoparticles in terms of swelling index, water solubility in polymer matrices, nanoparticle dispersion, and solubility in aqueous environments have demonstrated that these factors often affect the release properties of nanoparticles (Razavi et al., 2020).

The release behavior of R-CDs from CNF-based films was measured using water and ethanol solutions (10%, 50%, and 95%) as food simulants, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . For all films, R-CD showed rapid release within 1 h and reached a steady state after 2 h. In water and 10% ethanol, the CNF film showed a significant R-CD release. The CNF film's substantial swelling in water and 10% ethanol is thought to open up the structure and boost R-CD mobility and diffusion. The solutions can immediately and suitably hydrate the polar polymer matrix (Yuan et al., 2021). Consequently, R-CD migrated to this media through a shallow CNF matrix. Depending on the R-CD concentration, a progressive release process was seen using a 50% ethanol solution as a food simulant. The rate of R-CD release in 50% ethanol solution was modest, about two times lower than in water and 10% ethanol. Since the CNF film did not swell in 95% ethanol, R-CD was not released significantly. The closed structure of the polymer could be maintained by the limited access of 95% ethanol to the CNF matrix, which reduced the rate of R-CD release due to the trapping of R-CD in the polymer matrix and limited mobility and diffusion (Roy et al., 2021).

It is important to note that as the R-CD concentration increased from 1 to 3%, the release rate in all simulants increased correspondingly. Accordingly, the release of R-CD was fast and more in the CNF/R-CD3% film due to the high R-CD content in the polymer matrix. Ezati et al. investigated the release rates of CDs and nitrogen-doped CDs from CNF matrices using various food simulants. They found that CDs were released most readily from aqueous solutions, whereas nitrogen-doped CDs were released from alcoholic solutions. They claimed that the chelation of the cations reduced the polarity of CDs, increasing the lipophilicity and solubility of CDs in alcoholic solutions (Ezati et al., 2022). Similar findings were reported by Roy et al. for the release of turmeric CDs from a carrageen/gelatin composite film. They found that the CDs were released faster and to a greater extent in water than in alcohol solutions (Roy et al., 2021). As a result, the type of food simulation solution and the swelling ability of the polymer, followed by the affinity of the CD to the external solvent, are thought to affect the release rate of the CD.

The resazurin chemical structural changes at different pH levels cause color changes. In a preliminary test, the CNF/R-CD3% film showed a clear color shift near pH 7, so the color properties were investigated using this film under various conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the CNF/R-CD3% indicator film displayed color variations liable to the pH. Because the caproyl ester group protected the –OH of resorufin and prevented its color change in acidic settings, the indicator film was brilliant yellow under acidic conditions (pH = 4–6) and did not exhibit a substantial color change in comparison to the control indication film (Wang et al., 2021). However, at pH 7, a mild brownish-red color developed. The steady-state behavior persisted even as the pH was increased to more alkaline values. This discovery opened up the possibility for further biogenic amine detection using the CNF/R-CD3% indicator film. As a result, it is thought that the CNF/R-CD3% film's clear color shift, which is more visible to the human eye, is more suited for spotting food deterioration. The L, a, b, and ΔE values of the CNF/R-CD3% indicator film changed when the pH changed, as illustrated in Fig. 4b. Under acidic circumstances, the indicator film's brightness (L-value) did not change noticeably; for example, the L value was 73.5 at pH 4 and 71.3 at pH 6. However, there was a significant reduction when the indicator was exposed to alkaline conditions; for instance, the L-value was 70.3, 66.2, and 55.8 at pH 7, 8, and 10, respectively. As the pH rose from 4.0 to 10.0, the a-value dramatically increased while the b-value significantly declined, showing that the red and yellow colors grew brighter and weaker, respectively. As a result, the indicator film's total color difference (ΔE) increased significantly in alkaline conditions, indicating that the indicator has a visual and high-contrast response to pH.

Ammonia produces nitrogen gas that can be used to detect protein degradation in animal foods, making it a useful indicator for detecting food degradation. The ability of an R-CD-based indicator to change color in response to ammonia and acid vapors was investigated for its sensitivity to pH changes as a gas sensor (Fig. 4c). The instant the indicator came into contact with ammonia vapor, it became reddish-brown. The indicator did not significantly change color when exposed to acid gas, although it did have a faint, bright yellowish hue. Here, the ammonium and hydroxyl ions in the indicator film are hydrolyzed by the water and dispersed volatile ammonia, producing an alkaline indication surface. The indicator's color is then altered by the interaction of the hydroxyl ions with the phenolic hydroxyl groups (Zhai et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2019). Changes in the visible color of the indicator coincided with changes in the color parameters.

Fig. 4d displays the color characteristics of the indicator films subjected to ammonia and acid gases. The indicator film's b-value dropped from 20.1 to 6.2 while its a-value rose from 10.9 to 15.4, suggesting a progressive transformation of the film's hue from yellow to brown brought on by ammonia vapor. However, acidic vapors decreased both a (4.1) and b (8.7) values, indicating the yellowing of the indicator film under acidic conditions. The color response of the indicator film shows commercial applicability in smart packaging for meat and other products whose pH rises during aging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color stability of R-CDs-based indicator films
ce:section.ce:para: A pH-sensitive indicator's ability to retain color throughout its shelf life is important for providing consumers with real-time information about food quality. Changes in the color properties and ΔE values of the indicator film were quantitatively measured in daylight as a function of time to test the color stability of the indices over time. Table S2 shows the color values of the indicator films at the beginning of storage and after 60 days. The CNF/R-CD3% indicator film exhibited small changes in ΔE values and high color uniformity. A steady-state trend in ΔE values was observed between 0 and 60 days of storage, indicating good color stability of the indicator film. The results suggest that the developed CNF/R-CD3% indicator is very stable as the color performance of the indicator is reliable without significant change even after storage for 60 days at room temperature (22 ± 2 °C). The solid support's structural properties and the materials' caliber and purity directly affect the pH indicator's color stability. The porous nature of cellulose suggests that CNF can surround R-CDs to prevent and reduce oxidation by minimizing the effects of light, oxygen, and chemical structures and maximizing their stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of indicator for shrimp packaging
ce:section.ce:para: Processes involving biochemistry or microbiology cause prepared and perishable foods to degrade over time. Foods with high water activity, such as meat and seafood-based food, are more likely to experience microbial food deterioration. Usually, the proliferation of microorganisms causes these products to release volatile amines. The pH-responsive color indicator film's color changes show changes in pH brought on by the buildup of volatile amines inside the container (Kanatt, 2020; Morsy et al., 2016). The efficacy of the freshness indicator film was tested using shrimp packaging. Shrimp were packaged in a box, and the pH-sensitive indicator was positioned inside without touching the sample. It was then left at 25 °C for 24 h. The pH and TVB-N (mg%) values of shrimp as well as changes in the color values of the indicator film are depicted in Fig. 5 a and b.

The initial TVB-N of shrimp was 8.1 mg/l00 g indicating that samples were fresh in quality, which increased to 17.3 and 26 mg/l00 g at 12 and 24 h, respectively. Similarly, the pH value increased progressively from 6.7 to 7.0 and 7.4. Shrimp has a TVB-N threshold of 17 mg/l00 g; shrimp with a higher TVB-N cannot be consumed (Ghorbani et al., 2021).

The indicator film changed color as the shrimp began to decompose during storage, as shown in Fig. 6 a. The indicator film was initially yellow when the shrimp was still fresh but turned reddish-brown after 24 h. The indicator film's L, a, and b values have changed accordingly. The transition to reddish brown color was quantitatively confirmed when the a-value increased from 10.9 to 12.3 and 17.8, and the b-value dropped from 20.3 to 15.4 and 16.1 after 12 and 24 h storage. The ΔE of the film increased up to 11.2 after 24 h of storage. The color change of the indicator film in response to shrimp deterioration is categorically unique and easily identifiable even by inexperienced panelists due to the large ΔE values (Ma et al., 2017). The association between the TVB-N of the shrimp sample and the indicator film's ΔE was assessed using Pearson's regression methodology. It was strongly and highly positively correlated with a high coefficient of 0.93. Thus, we demonstrated that under alkaline circumstances, the CNF/R-CD3% indicator film's reaction is caused by CD, which stands for color intensity, and resazurin, which corresponds to pink. Under alkaline circumstances, functional groups on the CD surface cohabit with resazurin and create new complexes. Red resorufin dye and the distinctive proton signal of –OH is formed when ammonolysis removes the caproyl ester group from R-CD (Fig. 6b) (Chen et al., 2012). Therefore, the indicator exhibited a reddish-brown color and showed excellent visibility, especially in an alkaline pH region. This result paved the way for further studies considering the application of the CNF/R-CD3% indicator film for shrimp quality monitoring.

R-CDs were manufactured using an optimized hydrothermal process to create a pH-dependent colorimetric indicator using tangerine peel and resazurin. The developed indicator serves as an indicator probe to check the freshness of shrimp by preserving a number of oxygenated surface functional groups that can selectively react to pH changes and ammonia gas. The color change of the CNF/R-CD3% indicator film was revealed by the indicator changing from light yellow (pH 4) to reddish brown (pH 7). Based on its pH-sensing capability, this newly created indicator demonstrated excellent performance as a pH-sensing detectable damage system. In terms of the practical use of the proposed index in food packaging, it was used to measure the freshness of shrimp. A clear visual indication of the indicator can directly communicate poor food quality to the consumer. Color studies of the proposed indicator confirmed its compatibility with changes in important storage indicators such as total volatile basic nitrogen concentration and pH change. Using the proposed pH-sensitive CNF/R-CD3% indicator film to monitor the safety of fresh seafood could be a viable solution for the food industry throughout the supply chain.
Parya Ezati: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization.
Ajahar Khan: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft.
Jong-Whan Rhim: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding Acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare there is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (2022R1A2B5B02001422).
